steps.m: they (feminine) lower (their gaze) modestly (command to believing women)
steps.n: Added نَ to show this is for multiple women, connecting to the earlier mention of believing women (لِّلْمُؤْمِنٰتِ)
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects with أَبْصٰرِهِنَّ (their eyes) to show what the believing women should lower their gaze from.
Added a sukun (ْ) mark on the ن to show that it connects smoothly to the next word
Added vowels to connect the letters and make it into a noun meaning the ability to see
Changes the singular word for 'sight' into a broken plural form meaning 'eyes'
Adds هِنَّ to show these eyes belong to multiple females. The alif is written as ٰ (long alif) in this form.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added نَ to show multiple women doing the action
Added وَ to connect this to the previous command. This verb is connected to فُرُوجَهُنَّ (their private parts) which comes after it in the ayah.

steps.m: to split open, to create space between things

steps.n: Root meaning 'split/open' becomes a noun meaning a specific opening (private part)
Changes from singular to plural by adding 'u' sounds (like foo-roo-j instead of far-j)
Adds fatha to show this word is the object being protected in the phrase 'they guard their private parts'
Adds 'their' to show these private parts belong to the women being discussed
This is a negative particle that prevents something from happening. Here it stops the action of showing/displaying.
The وَ connects this negative command to the previous instructions about lowering gaze and guarding modesty
Added hamza at start to make it causative - showing that someone is making something happen. The final ا changes to ى in this form.
Changed from someone showing something to something being shown
Shows multiple feminine subjects
Added kasra to first letter to make it into a noun
Added ة to make it refer to one specific instance of decoration
Added هُنَّ to show the decoration belongs to them (feminine plural)
Removes the short vowel marks (zina becomes zin) but keeps the same meaning - 'their adornment' referring to women

This is a basic word used to show exceptions. In this ayah, it's used to say 'except what appears of it' when talking about women's beauty/adornment that can be shown. The word connects what comes before it (not showing adornment) with what comes after it (the exception of what naturally appears).
A relative pronoun meaning 'what' that connects to the verb ظَهَرَ (appears/is apparent) in this ayah. It's referring to the parts of adornment that naturally show.

steps.m: he appeared (part of ما meaning 'what/that which')
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. In this ayah, this word is part of ما which refers to 'what/that which' appears/is visible.
Basic preposition meaning 'from' - shows where something starts or comes from

steps.m: from it/her (referring to زِينَة their adornment)
steps.n: Added ها at the end to show this refers back to their adornment (زِينَة) mentioned earlier in the verse

steps.m: to strike or draw across something

steps.n: Transforms from base form to present tense verb. The ي shows it's happening now, but this is actually masculine form (she would be تَضْرِب). Means 'he strikes/draws'
The sukun (ْ) at the end changes this from a regular present tense 'she strikes' to 'let her strike' - showing it's now a request or suggestion

steps.m: and indeed let her draw (something across)
steps.n: لْ turns it into a command, telling someone to do something

steps.m: and indeed let them (women) draw (their veils across)
steps.n: نَ shows this is addressing multiple women, connecting to the earlier part of the verse about believing women

steps.m: and indeed let them (women) draw (their veils across)
steps.n: Connects this command to the previous instructions for believing women
The root letters that mean 'to cover' were turned into a plural noun 'veils' by adding the vowel marks ُ (damma) on both letters. This makes it mean 'multiple veils' instead of just one.
Added هِنَّ at the end to show these veils belong to a group of women. The ِ (kasra) connects it to the word.

steps.m: with their veils (used with وَلْيَضْرِبْنَ - and let them draw)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to show these veils are being used as instruments - the women are commanded to draw/strike with their veils over their chests.
This is a preposition that comes from the idea of being high or elevated. The root letters combine to make a word showing position above something.

steps.m: upon (connecting to جُيُوبِهِنَّ - their chest coverings)
steps.n: Added an alif mark (ٰ) to show this is a long vowel. This preposition connects to the word جُيُوبِهِنَّ (their chest coverings) to show where the head coverings should be placed.

steps.m: to pierce, make an opening, cut out

steps.n: Forms the basic noun meaning a place where something opens, like the neck or chest opening of a garment
Made plural by adding و and changing inside vowels to ُ (damma) - like many Arabic nouns do
Added هِنَّ to show these belong to a group of women
This is a negative particle that tells us the action of showing/displaying is not allowed
The و connects this prohibition with the previous commands about lowering gaze and guarding modesty
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The final و changes to ا because it's a weak letter at the end.
Changes from past tense 'showed' to present tense 'shows' by adding يُ at the start and changing middle vowel
The نَ at the end shows this is about multiple women. In this ayah, they are showing their زِينَتَهُنَّ (adornment).
Adding the kasra changes it from a verb (to beautify) into a noun (beauty/adornment)
Added ة at the end to show one specific instance of beauty/decoration
Changed form to connect with next suffix
Adds feminine plural possessive pronoun meaning 'their'

steps.m: except (what comes after this word is excluded from not showing their adornments)
steps.n: This is an exception particle that tells us what comes after it is different from the general rule that came before. In this ayah, it excludes certain things from the prohibition of showing adornments. It has no root since it's a basic particle.

steps.m: to be a husband, to master/own

steps.n: Changes from singular 'husband' to plural 'husbands' by adding vowels and و
Adds ة to make a noun meaning 'the state/condition of being a husband'
The word 'husbands' gets 'their' added at the end to show it belongs to the women (like saying 'the women's husbands')
The لِ means 'to' or 'for'

steps.m: or (connecting the list of people to whom women can show their adornments)
steps.n: This is a conjunction that connects multiple options in a list. In this ayah, it's used repeatedly to list all the categories of people to whom Muslim women can show their adornments.

steps.m: to nurture/care for (as a father does)

steps.n: Changed from root meaning 'to nurture' into the simple word for 'father'
Changed to broken plural form - this is a special pattern for making plurals in Arabic
Changed alif to long alif for proper pronunciation
Added kasra to show possession
Added feminine plural possessive ending to show these are the fathers of a group of women

steps.m: or (connecting the list of people who can see زِينَتَهُنَّ)
steps.n: This is a basic connecting word that gives choices between options. In this ayah, it connects a list of people who are allowed to see women's adornment (زِينَتَهُنَّ), like saying 'their husbands OR their fathers OR their sons...'
Root letters أ-ب-و become the simple word for father
Changes the word to show multiple fathers using a broken plural pattern
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) for pronunciation
Shows this word belongs to or is connected to 'their husbands' (بُعُولَتِهِنَّ) in the ayah

steps.m: to marry, to be a husband/master

steps.n: Connected the root letters to form the basic noun for husband/master
Changes the word to a broken plural form to show multiple husbands
Adds feminine plural ending to make it ready for feminine possession

steps.m: their husbands (referring to the believing women)
steps.n: Adds feminine plural possessive pronoun to show these are the husbands belonging to the believing women mentioned earlier

steps.m: or (connecting the list of people women can show their adornment to)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that shows options or alternatives. In this ayah, it connects different people that women are allowed to show their adornment to, like their fathers, husbands' fathers, etc.
Forms the basic noun for 'son' from the root meaning of building/creating
Forms the broken plural of ابن (son)
Extends the alif to show it's a long plural
Adds kasra to show possession
Adds feminine plural possessive pronoun هِنَّ meaning 'their'

steps.m: or (connecting the list of people who can see the women's adornments)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that gives multiple allowed options. In this ayah, it's used repeatedly to list all the different people who are allowed to see Muslim women's beauty/adornments.
Added vowels to connect the root letters
Changed from verb 'to build' to a related noun meaning 'son' (like how a son builds/continues a family)
Changed from 'son' to 'sons' using the broken plural pattern

steps.m: sons (possessed by or connected to something)
steps.n: Added kasra to show possession and lengthened the alif

steps.m: to be married, to be a husband/master

steps.n: Added damma on first and second letters (ب and ع) and inserted و between them to create the plural form following the فُعُول pattern, indicating multiple husbands as a group
Added ة at the end to change from 'husbands' to 'the state/condition of being a husband'
Added -i sound at the end to show something belongs to someone else
Added هِنَّ at the end to show the husbands belong to multiple women

steps.m: or (connecting the list of people to whom believing women can show their adornment)
steps.n: This is a basic connecting word that shows different choices or possibilities. In this ayah, it's used multiple times to list all the different categories of people that believing women can show their adornment to, like their fathers, husbands, sons, etc.

steps.m: to be a brother, to have brotherhood

steps.n: Root meaning 'brother' becomes plural noun 'brothers' by adding special pattern إِفْعَان
Changes from regular plural 'brothers' to dual form 'two brothers'
Adds feminine 'their' to show these are the women's brothers

steps.m: or (connecting the list of people to whom believing women can show their adornment)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that shows different choices or possibilities. In this ayah, it's used multiple times to list all the different people that believing women can show their adornment to.

steps.m: to build or be a son/descendant

steps.n: Adding vowel marks to make it a basic past tense verb
These are two different words - 'banaa' means built, while 'bani' means 'sons of' or 'children of'
Added elongation mark to show connection to the word that follows (their sisters)
Root أخو becomes إِخْوَان by adding ان to make the broken plural form meaning 'brothers'
Added هِنَّ with ِ connector to show possession by multiple females

steps.m: or (connecting the list of people who can see زِينَتَهُنَّ)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that shows different choices or possibilities. In this ayah, it connects a list of people who are allowed to see women's adornments.
Added vowels to connect the letters into a basic past tense verb

Changed from verb 'to build' to a completely different word 'banī' meaning sons/children. These are two separate words, not a transformation. The word 'banī' is connected to 'their brothers' in the ayah.
The ي and ending mark ٓ make this a plural form of the word for sons/children
Root letters connected with short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make the word for 'sibling'
The word for 'brother/sibling' gets the -ات ending to make it mean 'sisters' specifically
Adds feminine plural possessive ending to show these are 'their sisters' (referring to multiple females)

steps.m: or (connecting the list of people to whom believing women can show their adornment)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that links different options in a list. In this ayah, it connects the different categories of people that believing women are allowed to show their adornment to.
Changed the root letters into a plural word for women by adding vowels and a long ا
Added kasra to show this word is connected to another word that owns or possesses it (in this case, showing these are the women belonging to a group)
Added هِنَّ at the end to show these women belong to a group of females (like saying 'their women' when 'their' refers to multiple females)

steps.m: or (connecting the list of people to whom women can show their adornment)
steps.n: This is a coordinating conjunction that connects multiple options in a list. In this ayah, it's used repeatedly to list all the categories of people to whom believing women can show their adornment.
This is a connecting word that links to what comes next. Here it introduces 'whatever/what they possess' (ملكت). It's like saying 'what' at the start of a phrase to introduce more details.
Added fatha vowels to connect the letters and make it past tense

steps.m: she owned/possessed (referring to ما which is feminine)
steps.n: Added ت at the end to show the subject is feminine (she). This matches with ما which is treated as feminine in this context.

steps.m: to be on the right side or be blessed

steps.n: Added vowels to the root letters to make the word mean 'right hand' instead of the action of being on the right
Changed to broken plural form for multiple right hands
Changed normal alif to a special tall alif and added a u-sound at the end
Added feminine plural possessive pronoun to show ownership
This is a connecting word that links different choices or options in a list. In this ayah, it's used to list the people to whom women can show their adornments.
Added kasra at the end because this word is followed by the definite article ال in الطِّفْلِ (the children). This is a connecting sound that makes the words flow together better.
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Changed pattern to show someone who does the action
Added plural ending to show multiple followers
Added 'the' to make it specific followers
Added ending to show this word is the object in the sentence
Basic noun form showing something that is different or other than something else
Added kasra at the end to show this word describes التَّابِعِينَ (the followers) in the ayah
This is the base form of the word that means 'those who'. It's a special word that doesn't come from a root.
The small circle above shows this word refers to multiple people
Adding لِى at the end makes it specifically about possession or having something. In this ayah, it describes the word غَيْرِ (other than/without).
Basic root meaning of having a need or desire
Changes from general concept to a specific instance of need/desire
Adding 'the' (ال) makes it refer to a specific type of desire - sexual desire

steps.m: the sexual desire/interest (in relation to غَيْرِ - those without)
steps.n: Shows this word is connected to غَيْرِ (those without) in the phrase غَيْرِ أُو۟لِى ٱلْإِرْبَةِ (those who lack sexual desire)
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from
Added fatha at the end to help connect it smoothly to the next word الرِّجَالِ (the men). This is like adding glue between words to make them flow better when speaking.
Added vowels to make the basic singular noun form
Changes singular 'man' to plural 'men' using broken plural pattern
Adds -i ending to show these men belong to or are connected to something
Adds ال to make it specific/definite. In this ayah, it refers to the specific men mentioned in the context.
Basic conjunction particle that connects options in a list. In this ayah, it's connecting different groups of people to whom women can show their adornment.
When أَو comes before a word starting with ال, it gets an extra kasra (ِ) at the end to make pronunciation easier. Here it comes before التَّابِعِينَ (the followers).
Original meaning related to gentleness
Changes from verb to noun meaning 'one who is small/young'
Adding definite article makes the noun definite

steps.m: the child (connected to what comes after)
steps.n: The ِ shows this word is connected to what follows it in the sentence
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that points to someone or something
Added ين at the end to make it plural (more than one)

steps.m: those who (referring to children who haven't reached puberty)
steps.n: Added ال with shadda to make it specific and definite. In this ayah, it refers to children who haven't yet reached the age of understanding about private matters.
This is the basic negative word that tells us something did not happen
Adding مْ makes it specifically talk about something that didn't happen in the past. Here it negates يَظْهَرُوا۟ (they appeared/knew), making it mean 'they did not appear/know'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they appear/become visible (referring to children who haven't yet noticed women's private matters)
steps.n: Added وا with a small alif (۟) to show this is plural masculine. In this ayah, it refers to children who haven't yet noticed women's private matters.
This is a basic preposition showing position or location. In this ayah, it connects 'their head coverings' with 'their chests', showing where the coverings should be placed.
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation: 'a' sound (َ) after ع and 'a' sound (َ) after ل
Added small alif (ٰ) after ى to show it's a long 'aa' sound at the end

steps.m: to be hidden or private (past tense)
steps.n: Adding the fatha vowel mark shows this is a past tense verb
The sukun shows this is a basic past tense verb

steps.m: a private part (that should be covered)
steps.n: Added ة to turn the action of 'exposing' into a noun meaning 'private part that needs to be covered'
Changed to plural form to show multiple private parts or areas that must be covered
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to 'women' in the verse, and changed alif to alif maksura
Changed the root letters to make the special word for 'women' - one of the few irregular plurals in Arabic
Added 'the' to make it specific and doubled the ن to help pronunciation

steps.m: the women (being described in this verse)
steps.n: Changed to 'of the women' because it's connected to what comes after it. The long alif (آ) is just a writing style - same pronunciation.
Basic negative particle that stops something from happening. Here it tells the believing women not to do something.
Added و at the beginning to connect this prohibition with the previous commands in the verse

steps.m: to strike or draw across something

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense form
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they (women) draw (their veils across their chests)
steps.n: Changed to feminine plural by adding نَ and removing final ُ. In this ayah, it specifically refers to women drawing their head coverings across their chests.
Added vowel marks to turn the root letters into a noun meaning a body part
Added sukun to make the word flow better when speaking
Added أ at the beginning and damma to make it plural (more than one foot)
Adding ب means 'with' or 'by means of'
Adding feminine plural possessive pronoun suffix هن to indicate 'their' (feminine)
Adding vowel marks to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to know'
Changed the first vowel to make it passive - showing something is being done to the subject rather than the subject doing it
Added يُ at the beginning to show this happens in the present/future, and changed vowels to match

steps.m: so that it may be known
steps.n: Added لِ at the beginning to show this happens for a purpose

steps.m: that which (refers to what أَيْمٰنُهُنَّ possess)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun that introduces a clause describing what is possessed. In this ayah, it connects مَلَكَتْ (possessed) with أَيْمٰنُهُنَّ (their right hands), meaning 'that which their right hands possess'.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb
Added يُ at the beginning to show this happens in the present/future. The ْ (sukun) on خ shows where one sound stops and another begins.
Added نَ at the end to show multiple women are doing the action. This matches with the previous words in the verse that talk about what believing women should do.

steps.m: from (shows what is being hidden of زِينَتِهِنَّ)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows what something comes from or is part of. Here it connects what the women are hiding (their adornment)
Added kasra to first letter to create a noun meaning the result of beautifying
Added feminine marker ت to make the word refer to feminine beauty
Adds the feminine plural possessive pronoun suffix هِنَّ, with connecting kasra
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Changed from 'he turned back' to 'all of you turn back!' - a command for a group

steps.m: and turn back! (all of you) (connected to تُفْلِحُونَ - you may succeed)
steps.n: Added 'and' at beginning to connect with the next phrase about success
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination. Here it connects with the word اللَّهِ to show 'to Allah'
Changed from 'a god' to 'The God' by adding ال and merging letters. The first letter changes to ا with hamza dropped because it's the proper name Allah.
Added kasra at the end to show possession or connection. The ل gets a shadda to emphasize it. The initial alif changes to a wasl form since it connects to the previous word.

steps.m: to gather, collect, or bring together

steps.n: Added ي between second and third root letters to create a word meaning 'all together'

steps.m: all together (describing how تُوبُوٓا۟ 'turn in repentance' should be done)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to make it describe how the action should be done - everyone should repent together

steps.m: to point to or call attention to something

steps.n: Root letters أيي combined with fatha and shadda to make a word that calls out to someone
Added damma to show this is the subject form
Added ha and fatha to complete the vocative particle
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb about feeling secure
Changes the verb into a noun describing someone who does this action
Makes the word plural to show many people
Makes it specific by adding 'the' and shows it's the subject doing something
This is a particle that expresses hope or expectation

steps.m: so that you all may (succeed تُفْلِحُونَ)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show this hope applies to all the believers being addressed. This connects to the verb تُفْلِحُونَ (you all succeed) that comes after it.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added تُ at start and changed vowels to make it 'you/she' in present tense instead of 'he' in past tense

steps.m: you all will succeed (addressing the believers)
steps.n: Added plural ending to show it's addressing multiple people. This word is addressing all the believers (ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ) mentioned in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Adding أَ at the start changes the meaning from doing the action to causing someone else to do it

steps.m: he causes to marry (present tense, indicative)
steps.n: Adding damma (ُ) shows this is a normal statement, not a command or wish
Added وا at the end to change from 'he marries' to 'you all marry' - makes it a command for a group

steps.m: and you all cause to marry (command to arrange marriages for الْأَيٰمَىٰ)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect this command with previous statements
Adding vowel marks to show this describes someone's marital status
Changes from single to plural by adding letters to make 'ayama' - a special plural form for unmarried people
Adds ال to specify these particular unmarried people being discussed
Changes the writing style to use the elongated alif (ٰ) - this is just a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: from/among (preposition showing something comes from or is part of something else)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows something comes from, belongs to, or is part of something else. In this ayah, it's used to talk about who should get married from among the community.

steps.m: from among you all (referring to the Muslim community being addressed)
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show this applies to the whole community being addressed. This connects to the command in the ayah about arranging marriages for unmarried people in the community.
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Changes verb 'he was good' into 'someone who is good' by following a special pattern
Changed from singular 'righteous person' to plural 'righteous ones' by adding ين at the end. This ين shows it's plural and is being acted upon by the word 'marry' in the verse.
Added و (and) to connect this word to what came before it in the verse
Added ال (the) to make the word definite, meaning specific righteous people

steps.m: and the righteous ones (in accusative case)
steps.n: The fatha (َ) at the end shows this word is being acted upon in the sentence

This is a basic preposition that shows something comes from or is part of something else. In this ayah, it connects 'servants' (عِبَادِ) with the previous words, showing which servants are being talked about.
Root letters got vowels to make a simple noun meaning 'one who serves'
Changes to broken plural form to mean multiple servants
Adds كُمْ to show possession by multiple people being addressed
Changes root letters to make a feminine noun meaning 'female servant'
Changes from singular 'female servant' to plural 'female servants' with a different vowel pattern
Adds possession to multiple addressees
Adds conjunction to connect to previous phrase

steps.m: if (introduces condition for يُغْنِهِمُ - He will enrich them)
steps.n: This is a conditional word that means 'if' and introduces a condition. In this ayah, it connects two ideas: if they are poor (condition) then Allah will enrich them (result).
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form the basic past tense verb
Added يَ at the beginning to show present/future tense, and changed vowels to match present tense pattern

steps.m: they are/will be (subject of إِن if/when)
steps.n: Added وا to show multiple people (they). This verb comes after إِن (if/when) in the ayah, showing a condition.
Changed the word to describe a person who experiences the lack of needed things
Changes the word from describing one poor person to many poor people

steps.m: poor people (who are being described in يَكُونُوا)
steps.n: The word gets a fatha ending because it describes 'who/what they are' after يَكُونُوا (they are)
Added vowels to connect the root letters
Changed from 'became rich' to 'makes others rich' by adding yu- at start
Added pronoun 'them' as the object of the enriching. This word describes what Allah does for the poor believers mentioned earlier in the verse.
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the word for 'deity'
This is the unique name for the one true God in Islam. The ال makes it specific, the shadda emphasizes it, and the ُ shows it's the subject of وٰسِعٌ (All-Encompassing) in this ayah.
Basic preposition showing that the righteous ones (ٱلصَّالِحِينَ) come from among your servants (عِبَادِكُمْ)

steps.m: to give extra, to bestow favor

steps.n: Root letters arranged into a noun pattern with fatha-sukun to mean 'favor' instead of 'to favor'
Added kasra at end to show possession because it comes after مِن
Added هِۦ to show the bounty belongs to Allah
This is the base word - Allah's proper name. It's unique and doesn't come from a three-letter root like most Arabic words.
The ُ (damma) at the end shows that Allah is the subject doing something in this sentence - specifically making people self-sufficient (يُغْنِهِمُ)

steps.m: and Allah (as the subject doing something)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) at the beginning to connect this to the previous statement, and ٱ which is a different way of writing the ال at the start of Allah's name when it comes after وَ
Adding vowels to make a basic past tense verb meaning 'to be spacious'
Changed the verb into an active participle (someone/something that does the action)
Added tanwin damma to show it's indefinite and nominative. The alif is written as a long vowel mark because it's describing a divine attribute.
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to know'
Changed from past tense verb 'knew' to an intense form meaning 'all-knowing', by adding ي

steps.m: All-Knowing (one of Allah's names, describing His complete and perfect knowledge)
steps.n: Added tanween damma to show this is describing Allah in this context

steps.m: to keep oneself pure and clean

steps.n: Adding vowel marks to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: he seeks to keep himself pure
steps.n: Added يَسْتَ (yasta) at the start to show someone is trying hard to stay pure - like saying 'trying to be pure' instead of just 'being pure'

steps.m: and he seeks to keep himself pure
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect with previous phrase

steps.m: and let him seek to keep himself pure
steps.n: Added ل with sukun to make it a command/request
Added ن to make it refer to multiple people

steps.m: those who (subject of لَا يَجِدُونَ - do not find)

Added ال to specify this is THE one true deity, and the hamza was dropped due to frequent use. The word became a proper noun specifically referring to Allah.

steps.m: Allah (the subject of يَهْدِى - He guides)
steps.n: Added shadda on ل to emphasize the letter, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added damma to show this is the subject doing the action of guiding (يَهْدِى)

steps.m: to shine, illuminate, or give light

steps.n: The root letters are made into a noun meaning 'light' by adding the vowels ُوْ (oo sound)
Adding ل at the start shows this is for or to something
Adding kasra at the end shows this connects to what comes after it

steps.m: to His light (referring to Allah's light mentioned earlier)
steps.n: The ۦ after ه is just a pronunciation mark showing to extend the sound slightly. This word connects back to نُورُ earlier in the verse where Allah's light is first mentioned.
This is a relative pronoun used to refer to people. In this ayah, it refers to whoever Allah guides to His light, connecting to the next word يَشَآءُ (He wills).
Changed the middle letter ي to ا (alif) and added vowels to make it past tense 'he wanted'
Changes from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing the ending vowel to ُ
Adds a long vowel to extend the sound, common spelling variation
Added vowels (fat-ha) to make it past tense 'he did it'
Changes past tense to present tense by adding يَ at start, sukun on first root letter, kasra on second root letter, and damma at end

steps.m: and he strikes/hits/gives examples (subject: اللَّهُ - Allah gives examples to people)
steps.n: Adds conjunction 'and' to connect with previous statement. In this ayah, Allah is giving examples to people to help them understand.
Changed root into the basic noun form meaning 'a deity'
Added the nominative case ending to show this word is a subject
Added 'the' to make it specific/definite
Adding fathas creates a basic noun meaning 'example'
Changes from singular to plural by adding 'a' sound at start and making middle letter silent
Added the definite article 'the' to specify these particular examples

steps.m: the examples (object of يَضْرِبُ - strikes/sets forth)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is the object of the verb يَضْرِبُ (Allah sets forth the examples)
The root means being social, and was made into the word for humans/people since we are social beings. The ا shows it's a noun.
Added ال to specify these are the people being referred to in the example

steps.m: for the people (who Allah sets forth examples for)
steps.n: Added لِ at the beginning to show these are the people the examples are for, and ِ at the end because it comes after the preposition لِ
Connected the root letters to form the basic word for deity
Added shadda on ل to make this the unique name for the one true God

steps.m: Allah (as the doer of يَهْدِى - guides)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show Allah is the one doing the action of guiding in this ayah

steps.m: and Allah (as the doer of يَهْدِى - guides)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect this to previous statement, and added hamza with alif to help pronunciation
The doubled middle letter makes it mean 'everything' instead of just 'containing'
The kasra at the end shows this connects to the word شَىْءٍ (thing) that comes after it
The بِ at the start means 'with' or 'by'. Here it shows Allah is knowledgeable 'of' every thing.
The root letters combine to make the simple noun meaning 'thing' - one of the most common words in Arabic
Added tanwin kasra at the end to show this word is owned by كُلِّ (every/all) in the phrase 'every thing'
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to know'
Added ي between second and third root letters to show this is a permanent and complete quality
Added two dots (tanween) to show this is one of Allah's complete names/attributes
This is a basic preposition that shows location or containment. In this ayah, it connects to the word بُيُوت (houses) to show where something is located.
Connected the root letters with basic vowels to form singular noun
Changed vowels to damma and added و to make plural. This word comes after فِى (in) in the ayah.
Added two dots under the last letter to show it's receiving the action of being 'in' something
Added vowels to show this happened in the past. This word is connected to تُرْفَعَ (to be raised) in this ayah, showing who gave permission for the raising.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
Added ال (the) and changed the word to become the unique name of The One True God. The shadda (ّ) shows emphasis. The ُ shows this is the subject of the verb يُسَبِّحُ (glorifies) in this ayah.
This is a particle that connects what comes before with what follows, showing that Allah permitted these houses for the purpose of being raised. It works with the verb تُرْفَعَ that follows it.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: it is raised (houses mentioned in بُيُوتٍ)
steps.n: Changed to passive voice present tense, showing that the houses are being raised up. The تُ at the start and the vowel pattern shows this is happening to something rather than something doing the raising.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Adding يُ at the start and changing the vowels shows this is happening to something rather than someone doing it

steps.m: and it is mentioned (in بُيُوتٍ - houses)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) to connect this with what came before. This word describes what happens in the houses (بُيُوتٍ) mentioned earlier in the verse.
Basic preposition meaning 'in'
Added ها to show this refers to the houses (بُيُوت) mentioned earlier in the ayah. The ها is feminine because بُيُوت is grammatically feminine.

steps.m: to name, to mark, to be elevated

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Changes from 'to name' into 'a name' by adding alif at start and making middle letter silent
Adds damma at the end to show this is the subject
Adds pronoun suffix meaning 'His'. This word is the subject of يُذْكَرَ (to be mentioned) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning more intense

steps.m: he glorifies intensely (referring to the one who glorifies in the houses mentioned)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start, changing middle vowel to kasra, and ending with ُ
This is a prefix particle that shows direction or purpose

steps.m: for him/to him (referring to اسْمُهُۥ - His name)
steps.n: Added the pronoun 'him' to show who is being referred to. In this ayah, it refers back to Allah's name (اسْمُهُۥ) that was mentioned earlier.
This is a basic preposition showing location. Here it refers to the houses (بُيُوت) mentioned in the ayah
The ها suffix refers back to the houses (بُيُوت) mentioned earlier in the ayah. Since بُيُوت is a feminine plural word, we use the feminine 'them'.

steps.m: to go or come in early morning

steps.n: Changes from verb to noun showing the act of going early
Added the definite article ال to specify this particular time
Added preposition بِ meaning 'in' and changed the ending to kasra because it follows a preposition

steps.m: to be deep-rooted or come at the end of day

steps.n: The ي makes it into a noun describing a specific time of day
Adding آ at the start makes it plural, like how we say 'times' instead of 'time'
Adds 'and' to connect this word to what came before it (بِٱلْغُدُوِّ - in the early mornings)
Adding ال makes it specific/definite, referring to all late afternoons in general
Adding kasra shows this is connected to the word فِيهَا (in it) from earlier in the verse

steps.m: man, person (one who walks/stands on foot)

steps.n: The vowels make it a singular noun meaning 'man'
Changes vowel pattern and adds alif to form broken plural

steps.m: men (who are the doers of the action)
steps.n: Adds tanween dammah to show this is the subject doing the actions mentioned after it (not being distracted by trade, establishing prayer, etc.)
This is a negative particle that prevents something from happening. Here it negates the verb تُلْهِيهِمْ (distract them), saying business does NOT distract them.
The shadda (ّ) and fatha (َ) on the ل make the negation stronger, emphasizing that these men are ABSOLUTELY not distracted by business from remembering Allah.
Changed root letters to make 'he' form of past tense verb

steps.m: it distracts them (from ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ - remembrance of Allah)
steps.n: Changed from past tense 'she distracted' to present tense 'it distracts', and added هِمْ (them) to show who is being distracted. Links to what follows - they are distracted from remembering Allah.

steps.m: to trade or do business for profit

steps.n: Root meaning 'trade' became a noun by adding i-sound after first letter and aa-sound in middle to mean 'the activity of trading'
Added feminine ending ة to specify the particular activity or profession of trade, making it more concrete and specific
Added tanwin damma (ٌ) to show this is the subject doing the action in لَّا تُلْهِيهِمْ (does not distract them)
This is the basic negation particle that means 'not' or 'no'. It negates what comes after it in this ayah (بَيْعٌ meaning 'trade').
Added و at the beginning to connect this with the previous phrase. This connects the negation of بَيْعٌ (trade) with the previous negation of تِجٰرَةٌ (commerce).
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun representing the action itself
Added tanween to show this is an indefinite noun. In this ayah, it's mentioned alongside تِجٰرَةٌ (business/commerce) as things that don't distract certain people from remembering Allah.
This is a preposition that shows separation or distance. In this ayah, it shows what the business and trade are distracting them away from - the remembrance of Allah.
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make it into a noun meaning the act of remembering
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to Allah (اللَّهِ) that comes after it
Added shadda to emphasize the uniqueness and greatness of Allah

steps.m: of Allah (possessed by/connected to ذِكْرِ - remembrance)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show possession/connection. This word is connected to ذِكْرِ (remembrance) in the phrase 'remembrance of Allah'

steps.m: to stand firmly or establish something

steps.n: Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Changed to verbal noun (the act of establishing) by adding إِ at start and ا after second root letter
Added kasra at end to show connection to the word after it (الصَّلَوٰةِ - the prayer)
Added و at beginning to connect this word to what came before it
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters
Changed to a noun form by adding ة at the end. The و changes to a long alif sound (ٰ) because it's at the end of the word.
Added ٱل (the) at the beginning and doubled the ص with shadda to show it's a specific prayer

steps.m: of the prayer (owned by إِقَامِ - establishing)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end because this prayer belongs to the act of 'establishing' mentioned before it (إِقَامِ)
Added a long alif after ت to make it a verbal noun meaning 'the act of giving'
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this word to the words before it
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to الزَّكَوٰةِ (the zakat) that comes after it, meaning 'giving of zakat'

steps.m: to purify, grow, or make something better

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed the word into a noun by adding ة at the end. The alif above the و (ٰ) shows it's stretched in pronunciation.
Added ال (the) at the beginning. The ز gets a shadda because it's a sun letter that combines with the ل of ال.

steps.m: of the obligatory charity (connected to إِيتَآءِ - giving of)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to the word إِيتَآءِ (giving of) that comes before it in the ayah
Changed the root letters into a past tense verb by adding the vowel marks 'a' (fatha)
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and ُ at end

steps.m: they are afraid (of the Day mentioned in the next part of the ayah)
steps.n: Added ونَ to show multiple people are doing the action. This connects to the next part of the verse which talks about the Day they fear.
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun. The و takes a sukun because it's a weak letter.

steps.m: a day (object of يَخَافُونَ - they fear)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this is the object being feared. In this ayah, the people fear a day when hearts and eyes will turn about.

steps.m: to turn, flip, or change direction

steps.n: Added vowels to make it a past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter shows the action happens multiple times
Adding تَ shows the action happens to itself

steps.m: it is turning itself repeatedly (present tense)
steps.n: Adding another ت at the beginning shows the action is happening now

steps.m: it (the hearts) keeps turning itself over and over (in fear) (subject of يَخَافُونَ)
steps.n: Final damma shows this is the subject doing the action. In this ayah, it refers to hearts turning in fear, connected to يَخَافُونَ (they fear)
Added kasra vowel mark to show proper pronunciation

steps.m: in it (referring to يَوْمًا - a day)
steps.n: Added the pronoun ه with kasra to show 'it' refers back to the day mentioned in the verse where hearts and eyes will turn about
Forms the singular noun 'heart' - named because it constantly turns/changes emotions and states
The word for 'heart' changes from قَلْب to قُلُوب by changing the vowels inside the word to make it mean 'hearts' (plural) - this is a common pattern in Arabic
Added definite article and nominative case ending. This word is the subject of تَتَقَلَّبُ (turn about/change) in this ayah, showing how hearts will turn in fear on that Day.

steps.m: to see or perceive with the eyes

steps.n: Root letters got vowels to make the noun meaning 'sight' instead of the verb 'to see'
Made plural by adding 'a' at start and lengthening middle vowel - means multiple sights/visions
Added the definite article and nominative case ending. This word is the subject of تَتَقَلَّبُ (turn about/overturn) in this ayah.
Added coordinating conjunction to connect with previous word ٱلْقُلُوبُ (the hearts)

steps.m: to give what is deserved (reward or payback)

steps.n: Basic past tense verb meaning 'he gave/rewarded' - changed from root letters to a complete word by adding vowels
Changes from past tense (he rewarded) to present tense (he rewards) by adding يَ at start and changing middle vowel
Adding ل prefix indicates purpose ('so that', 'in order to')
Adding هم suffix as object pronoun meaning 'them'
Added ال to make it specific and changed the first letter, making it a unique name for The One True God

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of يَرْزُقُ - He provides)
steps.n: Added shadda to emphasize the ل, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added damma to show this is the doer of the action (providing)
Basic past tense verb showing a natural quality

steps.m: he did excellently (object of لِيَجْزِيَ - so that He may reward)
steps.n: Adding hamza (أ) at the beginning makes this describe doing something with excellence. This word is being acted upon by the verb لِيَجْزِيَ (so that He may reward) in this ayah.
This is a relative pronoun connecting to عَمِلُوا (they did) in this ayah. It helps explain that Allah will reward them for what they did.
Added vowels to make past tense singular form

steps.m: they did/performed (subject of لِيَجْزِيَهُمُ - so that He may reward them)
steps.n: Added plural masculine ending وا to show multiple people did the action. This word is part of what Allah will reward them for.
The kasra (ِ) sound in the middle shows this is happening in the present/future
The fatha (َ) at the end shows this is in the subjunctive mood
Adding هُم at end to show who receives the increase - refers back to the believers mentioned earlier
Adding و at beginning to connect this action with what came before - Allah rewarding them and then increasing them further
This is a preposition meaning 'from' or 'of'. It is a particle (حرف) and does not have a root.

Added shadda (doubled م) because it comes after the word أَحْسَنَ. This is a pronunciation rule and doesn't change the meaning. In this ayah, it connects to فَضْلِهِۦ (His bounty) to mean 'from His bounty'.

steps.m: to give extra, to be generous

steps.n: Root meaning 'extra/generous' becomes noun meaning 'favor' by adding marks (fatha-sukun-nothing)
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word مِن (from)
Added هِ at the end to show it belongs to Him (Allah)
This is a unique proper name that doesn't come from a standard root. It's believed to come from combining ال (the) with إله (deity) but has become its own distinct word.
Added shadda on ل to show it's pronounced strongly

steps.m: Allah (as the subject doing يَرْزُقُ - provides)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show Allah is the one doing the action (providing) in this sentence

steps.m: And Allah (as the subject doing يَرْزُقُ - provides)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this part to what came before it

steps.m: And Allah (as the subject doing يَرْزُقُ - provides)
steps.n: Changed the alif to a special form (ٱ) used when it has a connecting hamza
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, changing middle vowel to sukun, last two vowels to damma. This shows ongoing provision by Allah mentioned in the ayah.
This is a relative pronoun that connects two parts of a sentence. Here it connects to يَشَآءُ (wills/wants) to mean 'whoever He wills'.
Added fatha (َ) on م to show proper pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
The root letters ش-ي-أ became شَاءَ (he wanted) by adding vowel marks and changing ي to ا in the middle
Changed from past tense (شَاءَ) to present tense by adding يَ at the start
Added long alif to show extended duration of wanting/wishing
Basic noun form showing something being different
Added بِ (meaning 'with/by') at start and kasra ending to connect it to the next word حِسَابٍ, changing the meaning to 'without'
Changed the action into a noun showing the result of counting/calculating
Added tanween kasra to show this word follows بِغَيْرِ (without) in the ayah, meaning 'without any reckoning'
Starting with the basic demonstrative word that points to something
Added ال at the beginning and ي at the end to make it into a relative pronoun that connects descriptions to people or things
Added ن at the end to make it plural, showing we're talking about multiple people or things
Added و at the beginning to mean 'and'. This connects it to what came before. The word is followed by كَفَرُوٓا۟ (they disbelieved) which tells us what these people did.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb meaning 'he disbelieved'
Added وا at the end to show multiple people (they) did the action. Changed the last vowel to ُ (damma) because وا always needs damma before it.
Added َ (fatha) on first letter to show this is a past tense verb
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Changes from 'one action' into 'many actions' using a common plural pattern - like changing 'deed' to 'deeds'
Changes alif to alif maqsurah for writing style, no meaning change
Adds damma to show this is the subject doing something in the sentence
Adds pronoun هُمْ meaning 'their'. This word is the subject of the verb يَحْسَبُهُ (he thinks it) in this ayah.

steps.m: to move freely like flowing water
mirage (shimmering illusion like water in the desert)
steps.n: Forms a noun for something that appears to flow or shimmer like water but isn't real

steps.m: like a mirage (comparing to أَعْمٰلُهُمْ - their deeds)
steps.n: Adds كَ at the beginning to show their deeds are being compared to a mirage

steps.m: like a mirage (describing how أَعْمٰلُهُمْ/their deeds are worthless)
steps.n: The tanween (ٍ) at the end shows this is describing their deeds as something unreal and worthless, like when someone thinks they see water in the desert but finds nothing

steps.m: to be flat or spread out

steps.n: The و changes to ي because of the kasra before it. This forms a noun meaning the place where the action happens.
Adding ة makes it refer to one specific instance or area

steps.m: in a flat desert plain (location of سَرَابٍ/mirage)
steps.n: The بِ means 'in' and the tanwin (ٍ) shows it's indefinite (any flat area). This word describes where the mirage (سَرَابٍ) appears in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Added يَ at start and changed vowels to show present tense
Added هُ to show what is being considered. In this ayah, الظَّمْـَٔانُ (the thirsty one) is doing the considering.
Changes from verb 'to feel thirst' to the noun 'thirst'
Added ان ending to turn 'thirst' into 'very thirsty person'
Added definite article and strengthened first letter
Added nominative case ending to show this is the subject
Base form showing the root letters م-و-ه with the و converted to ا as a weak letter change
The و changes to ا and the ه changes to hamza (ء) because they are weak letters. This is a common pattern for words about liquids.
The long alif (آ) is added to emphasize the word, like saying 'real water' or 'pure water'
The ً (tanween) at the end shows this word is being used as an object in the sentence, describing what the mirage appears to be.
Connected the base letters with fatha marks to form the basic particle
Added shadda to strengthen the middle letter. This is part of the standard spelling of this particle.
Added the alif maksura (ىٰ) and elongation mark (ٓ) at the end. This is the standard Quranic spelling. This particle connects to إِذَا (when) in this ayah to mean 'until when'.

steps.m: when (followed by جَآءَهُۥ - 'he came to it')
steps.n: This is a time word that means 'when' and usually introduces something that will definitely happen in the future. In this ayah, it connects 'when' to 'he came to it' (جَآءَهُۥ), showing the moment the thirsty person reaches what they thought was water.
Added fathas and the middle letter changed to alif because it's between two fathas
Added a long alif to emphasize the action of coming
Added هُ at the end to show who the action was done to (him)
This is a negative particle that changes the meaning and tense of the verb after it (يَجِدْهُ/find it) from present to past negative. It's like adding 'did not' in English.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, changing middle vowel to kasra, and adding damma at end. The initial و drops because it comes between يَ and kasra.
Changed the final damma to sukun to show jussive mood (used after لَمْ)

steps.m: he finds it (object of لَمْ يَجِدْ)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show what is being found. This word follows لَمْ (not) in the ayah, so it means 'he does not find it'.
Adding vowel marks to make the basic noun pattern

steps.m: anything/something (object of لَمْ يَجِدْهُ - did not find it)
steps.n: Added tanween (double fatha) to show this is the object being acted upon. In this ayah, it's what was not found, completing the meaning of 'he did not find it [to be] anything'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. This word describes what happened when the thirsty person reached the mirage.
Added و at the beginning to connect this with the previous action (when he came to it). The و shows these events happened one after another.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. The shadda shows the doubled ل sound.

steps.m: Allah (as the object of وَجَدَ - he found)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being found in the phrase. Also changed initial alif to wasl form since it comes after another word.
This is a common preposition in Arabic that comes from the idea of being present with something
Root letters get vowel marks (ـِـ and ـْـ) to make a word meaning 'at' or 'with'
Adding fatha at the end to prepare for connection with next part
Adding هُ at the end to show this refers to Allah in this ayah

steps.m: to complete, fulfill, pay in full
to fulfill completely, pay in full
steps.n: Doubling the middle letter (ف) intensifies the basic meaning of fulfillment
Added attached pronoun هُ meaning 'it' as the direct object of the verb. This is a past tense verb with an attached pronoun.

steps.m: then He paid it in full (Allah paid the reckoning in full)
steps.n: Added conjunction prefix فَ meaning 'then/so'. The subject 'He' refers to Allah based on the previous part of the ayah.
Connected the root letters together with fatha vowels to make a basic word meaning 'he calculated'
Changed from a verb (to calculate) to a noun (the act of calculating) by changing the first vowel to 'i' sound
Added alif (ا) to make it into a noun meaning the result of calculating

steps.m: his account (being paid in full by فَوَفَّىٰهُ)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to mean 'his'. The fatha (َ) before it shows this word is receiving the action of being 'paid in full' from the verb فَوَفَّىٰهُ.
This is Allah's proper name, which doesn't come from a standard root pattern
Added shadda to emphasize the doubled ل sound
Changed alif to waslah alif (ٱ) and added damma to show Allah is the subject doing the action in this sentence

steps.m: and Allah (as the subject of سَرِيعُ)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this to the previous statement. Here Allah is the subject of سَرِيعُ (swift)

steps.m: very quick/swift (describing Allah's attribute of swiftness in ٱلْحِسَابِ)
steps.n: Added ي between second and third root letters with vowels to create an intensive adjective showing a strong quality

steps.m: very quick/swift (describing Allah as the doer of ٱلْحِسَابِ)
steps.n: Added ُ to show this is the subject/doer in the sentence
Changes from a verb (to calculate) into a noun (calculation)
Added ٱلْ to make it specific and ِ to show it belongs to Allah (since Allah is described as سَرِيعُ الْحِسَابِ - swift in reckoning)
This is a connecting word that gives a choice between options. Here it connects to the word كَظُلُمٰتٍ (like darknesses) in the ayah.
The root letters convey the basic idea of darkness
The root letters get damma vowels (u sound) to make it mean 'many darknesses' instead of just one darkness
Adding ٰت shows these are different types or layers of darkness

steps.m: like layers of darkness (describing the disbelievers' state)
steps.n: Added كَ at beginning to make a comparison and ٍ at end to show it's being used as part of a description
This is a basic preposition that connects words by showing one thing is inside another. Here it shows the darkness is in the deep sea.

steps.m: to be vast like a sea

steps.n: Root letters combined with a fatha and sukun to make the word 'sea'
Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) at the end because it comes after the word في (in). This shows it's being acted upon by the word في.

steps.m: to be deep or vast like an ocean

steps.n: Added vowels and doubled the middle letter to make it an adjective

steps.m: ocean-like, deep and vast like an ocean
steps.n: Added يّ to show it's like or related to something

steps.m: ocean-like, deep and vast like an ocean (emphasized)
steps.n: Added shadda on first letter for extra emphasis

steps.m: of something ocean-like, deep and vast (describing بَحْرٍ - sea)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra to show it describes the sea mentioned before it (بَحْرٍ)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels. The final و changes to ا because it's a weak letter at the end.
Added يَ at start to show present tense, with sukun after first root letter. The ا at the end changes to ى in present tense.

steps.m: it covers/overwhelms him (referring to how the wave (مَوْجٌ) overwhelms)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show who is being overwhelmed. The alif at the end is written with a special mark (ىٰ) when هُ is attached.

steps.m: to move in waves, to surge

steps.n: Changed the verb into a noun by adding fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter. This word is the subject of يَغْشَىٰهُ (it covers/overtakes it) in this ayah.
Added tanween damma to show this is an indefinite subject
This is a basic word showing where something comes from or what it's made of. In this ayah, it shows that one wave comes from above another wave.
The م gets doubled (shown by ّ) because it comes after certain letters. This is just for pronunciation and doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to be above or higher than something

steps.n: Connected the letters with fatha and sukoon to make a word meaning direction or location
Adding kasra shows this word is connected to something that comes after it
Added هِۦ to show this refers to something mentioned before (the wave)

steps.m: to move in waves, to surge

steps.n: Root letters م-و-ج became the noun 'wave' by adding the vowels. This wave is doing the covering action in the verse.

steps.m: a wave (subject of يَغْشَىٰهُ - covers it)
steps.n: Added tanween (double damma) to show this is an indefinite noun - meaning 'a wave' rather than 'the wave'
This is a basic word that shows where something starts or comes from, like saying 'the wave comes from above it' in this verse
The shadda (ّ) is added because this word comes before another word starting with ف. This makes it easier to pronounce smoothly.
This is a noun of place showing position
Added vowels to the root letters to make a word meaning 'above' or 'over'
Added kasra at the end to show this word is owned by or connected to something else (in this case, the wave)
Added هِۦ at the end to show this refers back to the wave (مَوْجٌ) mentioned earlier

steps.m: to pull along, to drag across
cloud (something that drags/moves across the sky)
steps.n: The pattern فَعَال turns the action of dragging/pulling into the thing that does this action - in this case, clouds that move across the sky. This word is in the nominative case as it starts a new clause in the description.
Adding the ٌ mark shows this is any cloud (not a specific one) and shows it's the subject of a new part of the description
Changes from a root meaning 'to be dark' into a noun meaning 'darkness' (like changing 'darken' to 'darkness')
Makes the word plural to show multiple layers or instances of darkness

steps.m: darknesses (subject of يَغْشَىٰهُ - that cover/envelop it)
steps.n: Adds the subject marker (ٌ) to show this word is doing the action of covering/enveloping in the verse. The alif (ٰ) lengthens the sound.
This is a noun that shows a portion of something
Added vowels to the root letters to make it mean 'a portion' instead of 'to divide'
Added damma to show this is the subject of the sentence
Added هَا to show this refers to the darkness (ظُلُمٰتٌۢ) mentioned earlier in the verse
This root carries the basic meaning of being in a higher position
Added vowel marks to create a noun showing location
Added fatha at the end to show this word is describing the location of the wave in the ayah
This is a noun that shows a portion of something
Connected the letters together and added vowel marks to make it readable
The tanween (ٍ) shows this word is describing part of something else - in this case, it's describing some of the layers of darkness mentioned in the verse
Added vowels and alif to create the conditional particle. This word introduces the time when something 'almost' (يَكَدْ) happens.
The final alif is lengthened to alif madd. This is a common Quranic style that adds emphasis and helps with recitation rhythm.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: he brought out (subject of يَكَدْ - he almost)
steps.n: Adding أ at the start makes the verb causative - instead of just exiting, it means making something exit. This verb is the subject of يَكَدْ (he almost) in the phrase 'he almost could not see it'.
Connected the root letters together. The final ي is dropped because this is a defective noun.
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of the verb أَخْرَجَ (he brought out)
Added هُۥ at the end to show possession. This word is the direct object of the verb أَخْرَجَ (he brought out).
This is a negative particle that makes present tense verbs negative and changes their meaning to past tense. Here it negates يَكَدْ (he could) to mean 'he could not'.
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation: fatha (َ) on ل and sukun (ْ) on م
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense form
The word changes from past to present tense by adding يَ at the start and dropping the middle letter و
Added sukun because لَمْ requires the verb after it to end with sukun
Added vowels to connect the letters together and make a past tense verb
Added يَ at start to make it present tense 'he sees'. The middle and end letters combine into one ى because they flow together when spoken
Added هَا at the end to show what is being seen (it/her). The long alif (ٰ) is added to keep the sound clear
This is a relative pronoun that means 'who' or 'whoever'. In this ayah, it refers to anyone who doesn't have Allah's light.
Added و at the beginning to mean 'and'. This connects to the previous description in the ayah. The whole phrase وَمَن is the subject of the verb لَّمْ يَجْعَلِ (did not make/assign).
This is a negative particle that makes verbs negative. Here it negates يَكَدْ (he could) to mean 'he could not'
The shadda is added for pronunciation because it follows a word ending in a vowel
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed to jussive mood by adding kasra at the end
This is a unique word transformation that happened historically to create the proper name for God in Islam. The ال was merged with the word in a special way.

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of the sentence)
steps.n: Added the final vowel mark ُ to show this is the subject of the sentence. The ٱ is just a different way of writing the initial ا.
This is a preposition that shows direction or purpose. In this ayah, it's connected to the word نُورًا (light), showing who the light is for.
Added هُۥ which means 'him'. In this ayah, it's saying 'for him there is no light' (فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن نُّورٍ).
Adding damma and connecting letters transforms the root into a noun meaning 'light'
Adding tanween alif shows this word is receiving the action of يَجْعَلِ (makes/sets). In the ayah, Allah is making/setting light for someone.
This is the basic interrogative word meaning 'what'

steps.m: then what/so what (connects to previous description of darkness)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this question to the previous description of layers of darkness
Basic preposition showing direction or purpose
Added هُ (him) to show who is receiving or who something belongs to. In this ayah, it refers to who is receiving or lacking نُورًا (light).
This is a basic preposition that connects words by showing where something comes from or its position. Here it shows that one wave comes from above another wave.
Adding shadda emphasizes the strength or intensity of the light
The tanween kasra shows this is indefinite (any light) and is possessed by or belongs to something

steps.m: A question word that expects a 'yes' answer and negates what comes after it
steps.n: This is a combination of three particles: أ (asking a question) + ل (for emphasis) + م (negates the present tense)

steps.m: to see with eyes or mind

steps.n: Added vowels to make the basic past tense form
Changed to present tense and second person (you)
The hamza and final vowel are dropped due to weak letter rules
The final ى is dropped because it comes after لَمْ which makes verbs end without vowels

steps.m: indeed/certainly (emphasizes that اللَّهَ is the subject of يُسَبِّحُ)
steps.n: Adding the shadda (ّ) makes this particle stronger. It introduces and emphasizes what comes after it. In this ayah, it emphasizes that Allah is the one being discussed in the following statement.

steps.m: a deity, one who is worshipped
steps.n: Changed the root into a noun for 'one who is worshipped'
Added fatha mark to show this word is affected by something before it
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter changes the meaning from physical floating to spiritual glorification
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added fatha to show this is connected to what follows. This preposition shows who the glorification (يُسَبِّحُ) is directed to.
Added هُ which means 'him/it', referring back to Allah mentioned earlier in the verse. This shows who is being glorified.
Added the small و mark (ۥ) which is just a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning
This is a relative pronoun that refers to all those who glorify Allah in this ayah. It connects to the verb يُسَبِّحُ (glorifies/praises) to show who is doing the glorifying.
This is a basic preposition showing where the glorification happens. It connects 'the heavens' (ٱلسَّمٰوٰتِ) to the verb 'glorifies' (يُسَبِّحُ).
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form basic past tense verb
The root word meaning 'rise up' becomes 'sky' by adding a long 'aa' sound in the middle and changing the last letter to hamza
Changes from sky to skies by adding وات at the end - a common way to make feminine plurals
Makes the word definite by adding ال, like adding 'the' in English

steps.m: of the heavens (possessed by/connected to something)
steps.n: Changes to show it's connected to something else - the kasra ending shows it belongs to/is connected to the word في (in/within) before it
The root meaning becomes a concrete noun referring to the earth or ground
Added basic vowel marks to connect the letters together
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific

steps.m: the earth (being acted upon by فِى)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end because it follows the word فِى (in/within) in the ayah

steps.m: and the earth (being acted upon by فِى)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect it with the previous word ٱلسَّمٰوٰتِ (the heavens)
Changed from action (flying) to the thing that does it (bird)
Added fatha and sukun to form the noun meaning 'bird'. This word is doing the action of glorifying (يُسَبِّحُ) in this verse.
Added و at beginning to connect this word with what came before it
Added ال to make it specific. The ل assimilates with ط making it doubled (طّ)

steps.m: and the birds (subject of يُسَبِّحُ - glorifies)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show this word is doing the action of glorifying

steps.m: to spread out or arrange in rows
steps.n: The root relates to arranging things in order
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb

steps.m: one who spreads out/lines up (active participle)
steps.n: Changed from verb (he spread) to noun (one who spreads) by adding alif after ص
Added feminine plural ending to match birds as a group

steps.m: spreading out wings in rows (describes الطير)
steps.n: Changed ending to match 'birds' (الطير) it describes, with long 'aa' sound at start
Adds dots under the last letter to show it belongs to/describes something feminine plural
The root relates to completeness
The shadda (ّ) doubles the middle letter to add emphasis to the meaning of completeness
Added double damma (ٌ) to show this is the subject doing the action in قَدْ عَلِمَ (has known)
This is a particle that adds emphasis or certainty to the verb عَلِمَ (he knew) that follows it. It makes the meaning stronger, like saying 'he definitely knew' or 'he has certainly known'.
Added a sukun (ْ) mark on the د to show where the word stops. This doesn't change the meaning, it just helps with pronunciation.
This is the basic concept of knowing or understanding something

steps.m: he knew (each one has known صَلَاتَهُۥ وَتَسْبِيحَهُۥ)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to show this happened in the past. In this ayah, it refers to how each creation has known its prayer and glorification.

steps.m: to pray, perform ritual prayer, or send blessings
steps.n: The word becomes the noun 'prayer'
Changes from the basic root to make the word mean 'prayer' by adding an alif and ending with ة
The word prayer gets a special ending (-ta) because it's the thing that was known/learned
Adds possessive pronoun suffix 'his'. The complete word صَلَاتَهُۥ is what is known (object) in the phrase 'each has known his prayer'
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Changes 'he glorified' into 'glorifying' by adding تَ at start and ي in middle to make it a noun showing the action
Added possessive pronoun suffix to show ownership. This word is what each creature knows (object of عَلِمَ)
Added 'and' (وَ) at the start and a small mark after ه to show it's stretched when reading
Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in the sentence. Allah is described as عَلِيمٌ (All-Knowing) in this ayah.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this part to what came before it in the ayah. Also added ٱ (wasl alif) which helps pronounce the word when it comes after و.
This form turns the action of knowing into the noun knowledge
Added vowels and ي to show this describes someone who has complete knowledge. This word describes Allah (اللَّهُ) in this ayah.
Added tanween (double damma) to show this is describing Allah's complete attribute of knowledge
Basic relative pronoun meaning 'what'
Added the preposition ب which shows how or with what something is done. In this ayah, it connects to يَفْعَلُونَ (they do) to show Allah knows with/about what they do.
This is the basic past tense form
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, sukun on first root letter, and damma on last root letter
Added ونَ to show multiple people are doing the action. This word relates to the multiple subjects mentioned in the ayah who are glorifying Allah.
Added kasra at the end to show possession or belonging
Added لِ at the beginning to show purpose or direction. The first ا is removed when لِ is added.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase to what follows (مُلْكُ - dominion/kingdom). The full meaning in context is 'And to Allah belongs the dominion...'
Changed the verb into a noun that means the power or authority one has. This word is connected to اللَّهِ (Allah) in this ayah, showing that all dominion belongs to Him.
Added the ُ ending to show this is the subject of the sentence, meaning 'the dominion belongs to Allah'
Adds اء to make it a noun referring to something high up
Added feminine plural ending ات. The ء changes back to و before the plural ending.
Added ٱل (the) at the beginning, which causes the س to get a shadda (doubling mark)
Changed to show 'of the heavens' by adding kasra (-i) sound at the end, with some spelling changes
Changed from a long alif (ٰ) to a regular alif (ا) in writing, but pronounced the same way
Added short 'a' (fatha) and sukoon marks to make it readable as 'ard' (earth)
Added ال to make it specific
Added وَ to connect it with 'heavens' mentioned before, and ِ to show it follows مُلْكُ (dominion)
This is a preposition that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it points to Allah, showing that to Him is the final destination.
Added وَ to mean 'and'
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the word for 'deity'
Changed to the unique name of the one true God. Added ال with a shadda on the ل, and kasra at the end to show this connects to what comes after it in the ayah.
Added مَ at the beginning to show where the action happens or ends up
Added ال to show we're talking about a specific destination

steps.m: the destination/place of return (subject of إِلَى ٱللَّهِ)
steps.n: Added ُ at the end to show this is the subject of the phrase 'to Allah is the return'

steps.m: A question word that expects a 'yes' answer, made up of 3 parts: أ (asking), لَ (for emphasis), and مْ (makes the verb negative)
steps.n: This is a combination of three particles that work together to ask a question

steps.m: to see or observe with one's eyes

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added تَ at start to mean 'you' and changed ending to ى because it's happening now (present tense)

steps.m: you see (after ألم meaning 'do you not')
steps.n: The ى is dropped because it comes after ألم which requires the end to be cut off. This word is part of ألم تر meaning 'do you not see?'
This is the basic form of the particle that introduces new statements
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger and more emphatic. In this ayah, it emphasizes that Allah is the one who drives the clouds.
Changed إِلَه to اللَه, which is a unique name specifically referring to The One True God in Islam
The word 'Allah' gets a shadda mark on the ل, starts with a joining alif, and ends with a fatha mark because it follows the word أَنَّ (that)

steps.m: to drive, push, or urge forward gently

steps.n: Added fatha marks (short 'a' sounds) between the root letters to make 'he drove' in past tense
Changed from past to present tense by adding يُ at the start. Shows Allah gently pushing the clouds forward in an ongoing way.

steps.m: to drag, pull, or trail along

steps.n: Added vowels and alif to make it a noun. This word is being acted upon by the verb يُزْجِى (drives/moves) - Allah is driving the clouds.
Added ًا (an) at the end to show this is the object (thing being acted upon) in the sentence

This is a connecting word (particle) that shows one thing happens after another. Here it connects 'He joins' (يُؤَلِّفُ) with 'He makes it' (يَجْعَلُهُ) to show these actions happen in order. Like saying 'He joins the clouds, then He makes them into a heap.'

steps.m: to join or unite things together
steps.n: Basic root meaning of joining or uniting things
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding shadda doubles the middle letter to show the action was done multiple times or with intensity

steps.m: he makes (something) join (Allah is doing this action)
steps.n: The يُ at the start changes it from past tense (he joined) to present tense (he joins). The ؤ is how we write the hamza when it comes after يُ. The ُ ending shows this is doing the main action.
Root meaning of separation or distinction
Changes from root to noun meaning 'between'
Added هُۥ at the end to show what is between. The ۥ mark shows the long vowel sound. This word shows where the joining happens.
Basic form of the connecting word that shows one thing happens after another
Added damma (ُ) and shadda (ّ) to show emphasis and proper pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the past tense verb form
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: he makes it (referring to the clouds mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Added هُۥ at the end to show 'it' as the object being made/set. The ۥ mark shows this is pronounced with a long vowel.
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb

steps.m: heap, pile, accumulation (object of يَجْعَلُهُ/makes it)
steps.n: Transforms from root into noun indicating large quantity. This word is the object (what is being made/turned into) in the phrase 'then He makes it into a heap.'

steps.m: into a heap/pile (object of يَجْعَلُهُ/makes it)
steps.n: The word gets اً at the end to show it's receiving the action of 'makes/turns into' - like saying 'into a heap' instead of just 'heap'

steps.m: to see or perceive with eyes or mind

steps.n: Changed the root letters into a past tense verb by adding the vowel marks 'fatha' (َ) to make 'he saw'
Added ت at the beginning to show 'you' and changed to present tense which drops the أ

steps.m: and then you see (connected to يُزْجِى in the ayah)
steps.n: Added فَ to connect this to the previous action (Allah driving clouds) in the ayah

steps.m: to fall in drops, to rain

steps.n: Adding fatha and sukun to create the noun meaning 'rain'. This is what is being seen in the phrase 'you see the rain'.
Adding ال makes it specific - 'the rain' instead of just any rain
Adding fatha at the end shows this word is being acted upon by the verb 'you see'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Changes past tense to present tense by adding يَ at start, sukun on first root letter, and damma on second root letter. This word is part of what يَكَادُ (it almost) is describing.

steps.m: from (showing the hail comes from within the clouds)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it specifically shows that the hail comes from within the clouds.
Added vowels to form basic noun from root
Changes from singular 'gap' to plural form meaning 'through/between' multiple things
Changed alif to alif maqsurah for writing style
Added kasra to show this word is possessed by something
Added pronoun 'his/its' to show what possesses the midst
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter shows the action happens multiple times or with extra force
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing middle vowel to kasra
Added و at beginning to connect this action with previous actions in the verse
This is a basic preposition that shows something comes from somewhere or is part of something
The sukun (ْ) changes to a fatha (َ) because it's followed by the word السَّمَآءِ (the sky). This makes it easier to pronounce.
